steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: “astronomers detected a striking and unusual phenomenon occurring in the constellation Coma Berenices, 4.7 billion light-years from ______ explosion of a massive star, also known as a supernova …” The blank sits between “Earth” and “the explosion.” What follows the blank is a noun phrase (“the explosion of a massive star”) that identifies what the “phenomenon” is — an appositive. A colon is the correct punctuation to introduce an appositive explanation after a complete clause.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Earth; the': a semicolon joins two independent clauses. “The explosion of a massive star …” is not an independent clause — it has no verb. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Earth, it was the': inserts “it was,” creating a comma splice between two independent clauses with no proper conjunction. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Earth. The': a period would leave “The explosion of a massive star …” as a fragment — it has no main verb. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Earth: the': a colon correctly introduces the noun phrase that explains what the phenomenon was. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
